alsoKnownAs: Fundulopanchax sjoestedti (spelling variant — sjoestedti/sjostedti orthographic only; accepted spelling per Catalog of Fishes is sjostedti)

nativeRange.habitat: Temporary swamps, raphia-swamps, and slow-flowing brooks in swampy coastal rainforest of the Niger Delta and adjacent southwestern Cameroon; shallow, heavily vegetated, shaded pools with soft, tannin-stained water

decorPreferences: dark fine sand or peat-based substrate
dense marginal and floating plants (Java moss, hornwort, Ceratopteris) to diffuse light
driftwood and leaf litter (Indian Almond Leaves) for tannins and cover
peat filtration or blackwater extract to soften and acidify

diet.notes: Piscivore-leaning predator in the wild, feeding on invertebrates and small fish. In the aquarium, primary diet should be live and frozen meaty foods: bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, and small earthworms. Dried/flake foods accepted reluctantly and should not form the main diet. May consume small fish and fry kept in the same tank.

goodWith: robust medium-to-large characins too large to be eaten
peaceful cichlids of similar size (e.g., dwarf cichlids kept in separate territory)
avoidWith: small fish or fry (will be eaten)
other male Blue Gularis (severe conspecific aggression)
guppies, tetras, or any fish small enough to swallow
slow-moving or long-finned species that cannot evade harassment

summary: One of the largest killifish in the hobby at up to 14 cm, the Blue Gularis is a spectacular non-annual West African species from the Niger Delta. Males display vivid blue, red, and yellow coloration with elaborate fin extensions. Predatory and territorial — must be kept with care regarding tankmates; a tight-fitting lid is essential.
careNotes:
The Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti) is among the most striking and largest killifish available in the hobby, with males reaching up to 14 cm and displaying spectacular blue-green iridescence on the flanks combined with red-and-yellow patterning and elongated caudal, dorsal, and anal fin extensions. Unlike the better-known annual killifish genera (Nothobranchius, Austrolebias), this is a non-annual species — its eggs do not require drying-down to hatch, and the fish itself is long-lived for a killifish at 3–5 years under good conditions.

Water quality is important: the species demands soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) in keeping with its rainforest swamp origins in the Niger Delta region of southern Nigeria and adjacent Cameroon. Peat filtration or blackwater extract, Indian Almond Leaves, and a dark substrate replicate the tannin-stained, low-hardness conditions of the wild habitat. Temperature should be kept at 23–26°C; cooler water within this range is associated with better longevity. Avoid temperatures above 27°C.

A minimum tank of 80 L (90 cm footprint) is appropriate for a pair or trio. Dense planting and floating cover serve dual purposes: diffusing light (the species prefers subdued conditions) and providing refuge for females, which males pursue relentlessly. A ratio of 1 male to 2–3 females is strongly recommended to spread harassment. Males are highly territorial with each other and should not be housed together unless the tank is very large with visual barriers.

This species is a confirmed escape risk — it is an accomplished jumper and will exploit any gap in the cover. A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable. Due to its predatory nature, tank mates must be too large to be eaten: robust medium characins, Corydoras, and Loricariids are suitable; small fish, shrimp, and fry will be consumed.

Note on taxonomy: fw-fish-278 (sjostedti) and fw-fish-279 (sjoestedti) are the same species — the difference is purely orthographic. The accepted spelling per Catalog of Fishes is sjostedti (this entry). These two records are merge candidates for end-of-slice cleanup. Multiple historical synonyms exist including Aphyosemion caeruleum, Fundulus gularis caerulea, and Fundulopanchax zimmeri.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-279 (Blue Gularis Killifish / Fundulopanchax sjoestedti). Original Lönnberg 1895 spelling "sjoestedti" simplified to "sjostedti" per Catalog of Fishes accepted form.
breedingNotes:
The Blue Gularis is a non-annual, substrate-spawning killifish. A dedicated breeding tank with a peat substrate or spawning mop is recommended. Keep the ratio at 1 male to 2 females to reduce harassment of individual females. Condition breeders on live foods (bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp) for 2–4 weeks before introducing the male to the breeding tank.

Females deposit eggs in peat substrate or spawning mops. Typical clutch is small per spawning event (a few to a few dozen eggs), though a productive pair may yield 50–60 eggs per week over a 2–3 week period. Eggs can be incubated two ways: in water (hatch in approximately 2–5 weeks) or semi-dry on damp peat (6+ weeks before wetting). Semi-dry incubation is reported to reduce fungusing. Up to 50–75% of eggs may fungus — antifungal treatment with methylene blue or acriflavine is recommended during incubation.

Fry are relatively large at hatching and grow quickly; they accept micro-worms and baby brine shrimp from birth. Fish do not typically become reliable breeders until close to one year of age. Breedability is rated moderate-to-difficult due to egg handling requirements and high fungusing rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti (Golden Pheasant)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12 cm / 4.8 in), temperature (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–20°H), minimum tank (90×30×30 cm / ~80 L), native range (Nigeria and Cameroon), habitat (swampy coastal rainforest), diet (small live/frozen foods), temperament (males aggressive toward each other), setup requirements (dark substrate, dense planting, floating plants, tight lid), and taxonomy (formerly Aphyosemion sjoestedti). Spelling variant sjoestedti used in URL/title — same species as sjostedti per Catalog of Fishes.

additional.name: FishBase — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (13 cm SL / 14 cm TL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Niger Delta, Nigeria and Cameroon), habitat (temporary swamps, raphia-swamps, slow-flowing brooks in swampy coastal rainforest), taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), diet (trophic level 3.4, invertebrates and small fish), non-migratory freshwater species, IUCN Endangered status.

additional.name: Aquarium Wiki — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Cross-checked care data. Site was access-restricted during this session; data supplemented from aqua-fish.net and killi.co.uk.

additional.name: British Killifish Association — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti
additional.notes: Specialist killifish source. Confirmed adult male size ~5 in (12.7 cm), temperature preference 70–74°F (21–23°C, noting higher temps shorten lifespan), pH 6.5, hardness 50–80 ppm DH, Niger Delta native range, bottom spawner, trio setup (1M:2F), 50–60 eggs/week over 2–3 weeks, egg incubation 5 weeks in water or 3 months damp-peat, males quarrelsome. Noted that fish don't become reliable breeders until almost one year old.

additional.name: aqua-fish.net — Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 11–13 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 4–18°N, minimum tank 75 L, lifespan up to 3 years, high-protein live/frozen diet, aggressive and territorial temperament, peat substrate spawning, incubation ~3 weeks at 23–25°C, 1M:2F ratio.

fish.breedingNotes: Non-annual substrate spawner. Peat substrate or spawning mop; 1M:2F trio recommended. 50–60 eggs/week; high fungusing rate (50–75%). Eggs incubated semi-dry on damp peat 6+ weeks or in water 2–5 weeks. Antifungal treatment advisable. Fish not reliable breeders until ~1 year old. Fry are large and fast-growing.
